unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: This last unit of Year 3 explores the world inside your head: thinking, feeling and imagining. You will learn sixteen words for ideas and emotions, including idea, sense, reason, sadness and experience. You will practise imagining, making a choice, giving a reason with because, and suggesting ideas kindly in a group. In the story The Box of Ideas, children at a school by the sea fill a special box with their dreams and hopes.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary section and learn a few thinking and feeling words each day.
Work through the six grammar mini-lessons, from abstract nouns to conjunctions and the simple past.
Read the story The Box of Ideas, then answer the comprehension questions.
Move through Reading and Listening, Speaking, Writing and Activities in that order.
Finish with the quiz and the self-assessment, then celebrate the end of Year 3.

visual.alt: A child imagining ideas and expressing thoughtful feelings

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A thought or plan that comes to you in your own mind.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I have an idea for a fun game we can play.
practiceSentences: Amal wrote her best idea on a small folded paper.
Sami had one idea, but he kept it hidden all morning.
Whose idea was the classroom reading tent?
Adam said, "I have an idea for our poster."
My best idea came while I was walking home.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside me, ask me to share one idea and explain where it came from.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To make a picture of something inside your mind before it happens.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I can imagine a big, colourful dragon flying in the sky.
practiceSentences: Sami likes to imagine flying high above the sea.
Close your eyes and imagine a silver river.
Can you imagine living on a tall mountain?
Maya said, "I imagine castles when I read."
I imagine my story before I write anything down.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: i - m - a - g - i - n - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Sit with my helper and ask me to imagine a place, then check that I described three things I could see.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The cause that explains why something happens or why you did it.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I have a reason for being late; my bus was slow.
practiceSentences: Every idea in the box carries its own quiet reason.
There is a good reason for every class rule.
What is the reason the birds fly south?
Theo said, "The reason I smiled was your joke."
My reason for saving money is a new book.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - e - a - s - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With a grown-up listening, ask me why I chose my snack today, and check that my answer gives a real reason.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The job that something is made for, or why a person does it.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The purpose of a pencil is to help us write.
practiceSentences: The purpose of the bright box is to hold our ideas.
The purpose of a lighthouse is guiding ships safely.
What is the purpose of a spider's web?
Sami said, "The purpose of practice is getting better."
My notebook's purpose is holding all my story ideas.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - u - r - p - o - s - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With my helper nearby, ask me the purpose of three classroom objects, and correct me kindly if I mix up purpose and reason.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The decision you make when you pick one thing from two or more.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: You can have a biscuit or an apple; it is your choice.
practiceSentences: Sharing his paper first was a brave choice for Sami.
Reading before bedtime is a calm choice on a busy day.
What choice will you make, football or drawing?
Dad said, "Take your time because it is your choice."
My choice for the trip snack is fresh fruit.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - h - o - i - c - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to stay with me, then give me two fair options and check that I explain my choice in one full sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A feeling inside you that helps you notice or understand something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I had a sense that it might rain soon.
practiceSentences: Amal wants to write music with the sense of rain.
The dark clouds gave us a sense of coming rain.
Do you have a sense of which way is home?
Maya said, "I had a sense we would win."
My sense of smell leads me to Mum's baking.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With my parent in the room, ask me which sense helps me notice rain coming, and help me use the word sense correctly.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The heavy feeling you carry inside when something makes you unhappy.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She felt sadness when her friend moved away.
practiceSentences: Talking about sadness can help another person feel understood.
Sadness filled the class when the holidays ended.
How do you chase sadness away on hard days?
Theo said, "Talking to Mum makes my sadness smaller."
I felt sadness when my best friend was ill.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - a - d - n - e - s - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With a trusted adult beside me, help me name a time I felt sadness, and remind me it is healthy to share that feeling.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Something that happened to you and stays inside your memory.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I had a fun experience at the zoo.
practiceSentences: Sealing the box was a strange new experience for everyone.
Riding a bus alone was a new experience for Nora.
What was your favourite experience this school year?
Adam said, "The boat trip was an amazing experience."
My first day at school was a happy experience.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - x - p - e - r - i - e - n - c - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With my helper listening, ask me about one experience I still remember, and check that I used the past tense.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Honest, and truly meaning the words that you choose to say.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She gave me a sincere smile when I helped her.
practiceSentences: Teacher Yasmin called the quiet paper very sincere.
A sincere apology comes from the heart.
Was his thank-you sincere and true?
Grandma said, "A sincere friend tells you the truth."
I wrote a sincere note to thank my teacher.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - i - n - c - e - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask my adult helper to listen while I practise a sincere thank-you, then tell me whether it sounded honest.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Kind, because you stop and think about what other people need.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She gave her friend a gift because she was thoughtful.
practiceSentences: It was thoughtful of Amal to help Sami try again.
The thoughtful boy saved a seat for his friend.
Was it thoughtful of Maya to share her umbrella?
Mum said, "A thoughtful guest brings a small gift."
I was thoughtful and carried Grandma's shopping home.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - h - o - u - g - h - t - f - u - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, ask me to describe one thoughtful thing a classmate did, and check that my sentence is complete.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Feeling that nothing will get better, however hard you keep trying.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I felt hopeless when I could not find my lost toy.
practiceSentences: One child felt hopeless while his father was ill.
The team felt hopeless until the very last goal.
Do you feel hopeless when a puzzle is hard?
Sami said, "I felt hopeless, but Dad helped me try again."
I never stay hopeless because I ask for help.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - o - p - e - l - e - s - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With my helper beside me, help me turn a hopeless sentence into a hopeful one, and explain the change.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: So pleasant that you feel happy while you are doing it.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Playing games with friends is very enjoyable.
practiceSentences: Amal thinks writing music would be truly enjoyable.
The trip to the farm was truly enjoyable.
Which game is most enjoyable at break time?
Nora said, "Baking bread with Grandma is enjoyable."
I find quiet reading very enjoyable after lunch.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - n - j - o - y - a - b - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult in the room, ask me which school activity is most enjoyable, and check that I gave a reason.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To let somebody do something, or to give them your permission.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My mother will allow me to play after my homework.
practiceSentences: Teacher Yasmin will allow every child to speak once.
The rules allow quiet talking during group work.
Will Dad allow us to camp in the garden?
The librarian said, "We allow four books each visit."
My parents allow me one sweet after dinner.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With my grown-up helper listening, ask me what my family will allow after homework, and check that I used will allow.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To offer an idea or a plan for other people to consider.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I suggest we play outside after lunch.
practiceSentences: Please suggest one idea for our new class garden.
Teachers suggest reading a little every single night.
What game do you suggest for break time?
Adam said, "I suggest we start with the puzzle."
I suggest mangoes for our class fruit salad.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - u - g - g - e - s - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to sit with me, then let me suggest three ideas for a class garden and pick the strongest one.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To like doing something so much that you want more of it.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I enjoy reading stories about animals.
practiceSentences: Sami and Amal enjoy walking home along the shore.
The children enjoy story time under the big tree.
Do you enjoy walks along the beach?
Maya said, "I enjoy helping in the kitchen."
I enjoy quiet mornings with my colouring book.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With my helper nearby, ask me two things I enjoy doing after school, and check that both answers use enjoy.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To feel sure inside that something is true or right.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I believe that kindness makes the world better.
practiceSentences: Sami now believes that imagination is a kind of flying.
We believe honesty is better than winning.
Do you believe our team can win tomorrow?
Theo said, "I believe you can solve it."
I believe my little sister will read soon.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - e - l - i - e - v - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With a trusted adult listening, ask me one thing I believe about kindness, and help me add a because reason.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use abstract nouns like idea, reason, experience, and sadness to talk about thoughts and feelings.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: All sixteen unit words used correctly in the vocabulary tasks, and four abstract nouns named in the Grammar 1 practice.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Say what you imagine and describe the pictures in your mind.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: One spoken description of something imagined in Speaking 3, plus the imagination recount written for Writing 2.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Give a reason for something using because.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Three sentences completed with because in Activity 3, each giving a reason that truly fits the first half.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use modal verbs like can and will to talk about what is possible and what you plan to do.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Correct modal choices across the Grammar 2 practice, and one will sentence used aloud in Speaking 1.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Suggest ideas and allow others to speak in a group.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: One suggestion made and one classmate invited to speak during the class garden discussion.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Tell someone what another person said, using reported speech.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Four sentences rewritten accurately in the Grammar 5 reported-speech practice, with pronoun and tense both moved.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Answer opinion questions such as “What do you believe?”
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: One three-sentence opinion answer in Writing 3 that states a belief and supports it with a reason.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Write about a feeling, a memory, or something you imagine.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: One finished Box of Ideas paper in Writing 1 describing a dream, a memory, or a hope, revised after feedback.

readings.title: The Box of Ideas at School
readings.setting: A seaside town: the school, the shore and home
readings.passageScript: Teacher Yasmin brought a bright box into class this morning. “This is our Box of Ideas,” she said. “Every child will add one idea. It can be a dream, a memory, or a hope.” My friend Sami had a good idea. He was not sure about its purpose. “Imagine something better,” I told him. So he closed his eyes and thought for a while. His idea was about flying high over the sea. It was a thoughtful and sincere idea, and the whole class smiled.

readings.title: Feelings Are Not Bad or Good
readings.setting: A seaside town: the school, the shore and home
readings.passageScript: Every day we feel many different things inside. Sometimes we feel joy, and sometimes we feel sadness. Sadness is not a bad thing; it is simply a feeling. Rain is a kind of weather, and sadness is a kind of feeling. When Nora felt hopeless because her cat was lost, she talked to her mother. Her mother listened with a sincere heart and did not rush her. “It is good to share how you feel,” she said. “When we talk about sadness, we help each other feel understood.” Soon Nora felt hopeful again, and she thanked her mother.

readings.title: A Group Discussion
readings.setting: A seaside town: the school, the shore and home
readings.passageScript:
Teacher Yasmin: “Today we plan our class garden. Please suggest an idea, and allow everyone to speak.”
Leo: “I suggest we grow tomatoes, because we can eat them at lunch.”
Maya: “That is a good idea. I think we should grow flowers too, so the garden looks beautiful.”
Teacher Yasmin: “Thank you both. Those are thoughtful choices.”

readings.title: What Sami Said
readings.setting: A seaside town: the school, the shore and home
readings.passageScript: Sami said, “Sometimes I imagine that I can fly.” Later, his friend Amal told her mother about it. “Sami said he could imagine flying,” she explained. “He said it made him feel free and calm.”

readings.title: The Box of Ideas
readings.setting: A seaside town: the school, the shore and home
readings.passageScript:
At a school by the sea, Teacher Yasmin stood at the front. She was holding a bright, colourful box. The children leaned forward to look.
“This,” she said, “is our Box of Ideas. Each of you will add one idea. It can be a dream, a memory, or a hope.”
“Why?” asked Sami.
“Because ideas help us grow,” Teacher Yasmin said. “They come from feeling, thinking, and listening. Every idea has a reason.”
The next day, Sami sat quietly under a tree in the schoolyard. He wrote something on a small paper and folded it twice. Then he looked around, but he did not smile.
“What is the matter?” asked his classmate, Amal.
“I don’t think my idea is good,” said Sami. “I don’t even know its purpose.”
“Try again,” she said kindly. “Imagine something better.”
Amal showed him her paper: “One day I will write music with the sense of rain and drums. It will be enjoyable, like dancing on the beach.”
When Sami placed his paper in the box, he still felt unsure.
Later, the whole class sat together in a circle. Teacher Yasmin opened the box. She read the first paper: “I want a garden where children feel peace.”
“What a lovely choice,” said Amal. “That is so thoughtful.”
The teacher read another paper: “I would allow everyone to play, no matter what.”
“That shows a kind purpose,” said Teacher Yasmin, smiling.
Then she read one more: “I once felt hopeless when my father was ill. But he got better, and now I plant trees with him.” The room went very quiet.
“That is a very sincere idea,” the teacher said softly.
Amal nodded. “That story has a strong reason.”
At last the teacher reached Sami’s paper. She read: “Sometimes I imagine that I can fly. I would fly over the sea and wave at my grandfather. I don’t know what it means, but I like the feeling.”
The whole class smiled. Amal clapped softly. “That is the best idea yet,” she said.
After class, Teacher Yasmin said, “Sami, you shared something thoughtful. That is what imagination is for.” Sami smiled at last.
That evening, Amal and Sami walked home together along the shore. “I suggest we make a music box next,” said Amal.
“What would it play?” asked Sami.
“Whatever we feel,” she said.
The next morning, the teacher asked the class a question. “What do you believe? Should we share our ideas, even when we feel sadness?”
“Yes,” said Sami. “Sadness can help other people feel understood.”
Teacher Yasmin smiled. “Then you have all learned something important.”
The children wrote in their journals. Sami wrote: “I learned that a small experience can still have a strong reason. I will keep writing and imagining.” Amal wrote: “I will try to be more sincere when I share.”
At the end of the week, the box was full. “Now we will seal it and open it again next year,” the teacher said.
“Why?” asked Sami again.
“Because then you will see how your ideas and choices have grown.”
The children walked out into the warm afternoon. Sami whispered, “I used to feel unsure. Now I believe that imagination is a kind of flying.”
Amal laughed. “And we can fly at any time,” she said, “with a good idea.”

comprehension.question: What did Teacher Yasmin bring into class, and what did she ask each child to add?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She brought a bright box called the Box of Ideas, and each child adds one idea to it.
comprehension.explanation: The opening two sentences name the box and state that every child will add one idea.
comprehension.origin: Authored from the Grade 3 Lesson and Story source

comprehension.question: Which three kinds of idea does the text say a child may put into the box?
comprehension.correctAnswer: A dream, a memory, or a hope.
comprehension.explanation: The teacher lists exactly those three kinds when she explains what the box is for.
comprehension.origin: Authored from the Grade 3 Lesson and Story source

comprehension.question: What does the writer compare sadness with, and what does the comparison show?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The writer compares sadness with rain, showing that sadness is only one kind of feeling.
comprehension.explanation: The text says rain is a kind of weather, so sadness is a kind of feeling rather than something bad.
comprehension.origin: Authored from the Grade 3 Lesson and Story source

comprehension.question: Why did Nora feel hopeless, and who listened to her?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Nora felt hopeless because her cat was lost, and her mother listened to her.
comprehension.explanation: The text gives the lost cat as the cause and says her mother listened with a sincere heart.
comprehension.origin: Authored from the Grade 3 Lesson and Story source

comprehension.question: What did Leo suggest for the class garden, and what reason did he give?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Leo suggested growing tomatoes, because the class can eat them at lunch.
comprehension.explanation: Leo joins his suggestion to his reason with because, so the class hears why tomatoes are useful.
comprehension.origin: Authored from the Grade 3 Lesson and Story source

comprehension.question: Which two words change when Amal reports what Sami said about flying?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The word I becomes he, and the word can becomes could.
comprehension.explanation: Sami speaks as I can imagine, and Amal reports it as he could imagine, so the pronoun and the modal both move.
comprehension.origin: Authored from the Grade 3 Lesson and Story source

comprehension.question: Teacher Yasmin gives the class two instructions. What are they, and how do they help a discussion?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She says to suggest an idea and to allow everyone to speak, so every child gets a fair turn.
comprehension.explanation: Both instructions begin with a plain verb, and together they keep one voice from filling the whole discussion.
comprehension.origin: Authored from the Grade 3 Lesson and Story source

comprehension.question: Why does Amal say he could imagine flying instead of I can imagine flying?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Because she is reporting what Sami said rather than speaking as Sami herself.
comprehension.explanation: In reported speech the speaker changes, so the pronoun moves to he and the present can moves back to could.
comprehension.origin: Authored from the Grade 3 Lesson and Story source

comprehension.question: How did Sami feel after he folded his paper, and which detail shows it?
comprehension.correctAnswer: He still felt unsure, and the story says he looked around but did not smile.
comprehension.explanation: The narrator shows the feeling through his actions, and repeats it when Sami places the paper in the box.
comprehension.origin: Authored from the Grade 3 Lesson and Story source

comprehension.question: Which paper made the room go very quiet, and why do you think it did?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The paper about a father who was ill, because the writer shared a real and painful memory.
comprehension.explanation: The class hears an honest experience, and the teacher answers the silence by calling the idea very sincere.
comprehension.origin: Authored from the Grade 3 Lesson and Story source

comprehension.question: What was Sami's own idea, and how did the class show what they thought of it?
comprehension.correctAnswer: He imagined flying over the sea and waving at his grandfather, and the class smiled while Amal clapped.
comprehension.explanation: The story quotes his paper and then gives the reaction, so we know the class valued his idea.
comprehension.origin: Authored from the Grade 3 Lesson and Story source

comprehension.question: Why does Teacher Yasmin want to seal the box and open it again next year?
comprehension.correctAnswer: So the children can see how their own ideas and choices have grown.
comprehension.explanation: She gives that reason herself the second time Sami asks why.
comprehension.origin: Authored from the Grade 3 Lesson and Story source

grammar.title: Abstract Nouns
grammar.explanation: An abstract noun names something you cannot see, touch, or hold, such as a feeling, a quality, or an idea. Words like idea, experience, sadness and kindness are all abstract nouns. They let you talk about what is happening inside your mind and heart.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Name the feeling or idea: “She shared an idea.”
Add a describing word to make it stronger: “She shared a brilliant idea.”
More examples: “I had a strange experience.” · “His kindness made a difference.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Learners sometimes forget that a feeling word is a noun and write “She is very sad feeling.” ✗
Use the abstract noun instead: “She felt great sadness.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Abstract nouns are like clouds: you cannot touch one, but you can still see its shape. Ask yourself, “Can I touch it?” If you cannot, it is probably an abstract noun.
grammar.practice: Fill in each blank with an abstract noun from the word bank: courage, happiness, idea, sadness. | His ______ helped him face his fears. | She felt pure ______ when she heard the good news. | Teacher Yasmin praised the brilliant ______ in that paper. | He felt deep ______ when his friend moved away.

grammar.title: Modal Verbs (can, will, should, must) and the Future with “Will”
grammar.explanation: Modal verbs are small helper verbs that show what is possible, what you are able to do, what you are allowed to do, or what you plan to do. The modal will also carries your promises and predictions about the future.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use this pattern: subject + modal verb + plain verb, with no “s” and no “to”.
can means able to: “I can imagine a better world.”
should means a good idea: “We should help the new student.”
must means something you have to do: “You must listen carefully.”
will points to the future: “We will share more ideas tomorrow.”
Negative: add not, or shorten it to won't: “I will not forget.”
Question: put will first: “Will you come to the concert?”
grammar.commonMistake:
It is tempting to add an extra “s”, or to keep the -ing form: “He cans swim.” / “I will going.” ✗
Modals never change and the verb after them stays plain: “He can swim.” / “I will go.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: A modal is a helper who always walks beside a plain verb. Helpers never wear an “s”, and they never bring “to” along with them.
grammar.practice: Choose the best modal verb. | You ______ (can / must) go to bed early tonight, because it is a rule. | We ______ (could / should) try to help the new student. | One day, people ______ (will / must) share more ideas. | ______ (Can / Should) you imagine a world with no sadness? | Now complete each sentence with will and the verb in brackets. | She ______ (play) the piano at the concert. | They ______ (not / go) to the beach tomorrow. | I ______ (help) you with your homework.

grammar.title: Imperatives for Instructions, and Opinion Questions
grammar.explanation: An imperative gives an instruction, a command, or a friendly request, and it always starts with the plain verb. An opinion question does the opposite job: it invites somebody else to tell you what they think or feel.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Start with the plain verb and leave out “you”: “Allow others to speak.”
Add details to make it clear: “Suggest a helpful idea for the project.”
For a do-not command, begin with Don't: “Don't interrupt when someone is talking.”
To be polite, add please: “Please listen carefully.”
Open an opinion with What: “What do you think about school?”
Ask a yes-or-no opinion with Is: “Is sadness always a bad thing?”
Ask about a belief with Do: “Do you believe in kindness?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Two habits spoil these sentences: adding “you” to a command, and dropping the question word. “You allowing others to speak.” / “You like the idea?” ✗
Write “Allow others to speak.” and “Do you like the idea?” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: A command opens with a doing word; an opinion question opens with What, Is, Should or Do. Check the very first word and you will know which sentence you have built.
grammar.practice: Write the correct imperative using the verb in brackets. | ______ (help) your classmates when they ask. | ______ (not interrupt) when someone else is talking. | ______ (share) your ideas with the group. | ______ (be) respectful to everyone. | Now choose the correct question word. | ______ (What / Where) do you think about the summer holidays? | ______ (Is / Are) homework always necessary? | ______ (Do / Did) you believe that everyone should be allowed to play?

grammar.title: Countable and Uncountable Nouns
grammar.explanation: Countable nouns are things you can count one by one, like one idea and then two ideas. Uncountable nouns cannot be split into single pieces, like sadness or water. Knowing the difference helps you choose between how much and how many.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Countable nouns take a / an, a number, or many: “a reason”, “three ideas”, “many choices”.
Uncountable nouns take some or much, and never a / an: “some water”, “much experience”.
Countable nouns add s in the plural, so one idea becomes two ideas, while uncountable nouns never change.
grammar.commonMistake:
Sentences such as “I have a water.” or “She has much ideas.” mix the two groups up. ✗
Water is uncountable and ideas are countable: “I have some water.” / “She has many ideas.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Picture dropping the noun into a bag. If you can count the pieces as they fall in, it is countable; if it pours in like liquid, it is uncountable.
grammar.practice: Choose the correct words. | I have ______ (a reason / some reasons) to be happy. | There is ______ (many water / some water) in the glass. | She has ______ (three experiences / much experience) with drawing.

grammar.title: Reported Speech
grammar.explanation: Reported speech is the way you tell somebody what another person said, without using their exact words. Instead of “He said, ‘I feel hopeless,’” you say, “He said he felt hopeless.” It lets you share other people's ideas and feelings.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Begin with a reporting verb such as said or told.
Change the pronoun so it fits the new speaker: “I” becomes “he” or “she”.
Move the verb back into the past: “feel” becomes “felt”, “can” becomes “could”, “will” becomes “would”.
Take the quotation marks away.
grammar.commonMistake:
The quickest slip is keeping the present tense and the speech marks: “She said, ‘My choice is thoughtful.’” ✗
Move the verb back and drop the marks: “She said her choice was thoughtful.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Count three moves every single time: change the pronoun, change the tense, and drop the quotation marks.
grammar.practice: Rewrite each sentence in reported speech. | Direct: “I love ice cream.” → Reported: He said ______. | Direct: “She will come to the game.” → Reported: She said ______. | Direct: “We are happy.” → Reported: They said ______. | Direct: “I can imagine flying.” → Reported: Sami said ______.

grammar.title: Conjunctions (and, but, or, so, because) and the Simple Past in Stories
grammar.explanation: Conjunctions are joining words that glue two ideas into one flowing sentence, and the most useful ones are and, but, or, so and because. Stories also need the simple past tense, which tells the reader that the actions are already finished.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
and adds: “I like thinking and dreaming.”
but shows a difference: “I like the idea, but it is hard.”
or offers a choice: “Do you want juice or milk?”
so shows a result: “It was raining, so we stayed inside.”
because gives a reason: “I enjoy thinking because it helps me learn.”
Most past-tense verbs add -ed: imagine becomes imagined · allow becomes allowed · play becomes played.
Some verbs are irregular and change shape: go becomes went · see becomes saw · have becomes had.
grammar.commonMistake:
Choosing the wrong glue changes the meaning: “I enjoy thinking, but it helps me learn.” ✗
Because gives a reason: “I enjoy thinking because it helps me learn.” ✓ Watch the past tense too: “He goed to the shop.” should be “He went to the shop.”
grammar.memoryTip: Match the glue to the job: adding, contrasting, choosing, result or reason. For the past tense, try -ed first, then check the short list of irregular verbs.
grammar.practice: Fill in each blank with: and, but, or, so, or because. | I like video games, ______ my brother prefers board games. | We wanted to go to the park, ______ it was too hot. | He studied all evening, ______ he wanted to pass the test. | She enjoys painting ______ it helps her relax. | Now write the simple past of the verb in brackets. | Yesterday, she ______ (imagine) a magical land. | Last week, he ______ (allow) his friend to join the game. | They ______ (see) a beautiful sunset.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Share an Idea
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Get ready to say your idea and your reason:
“I have an idea. I suggest we ______.”
“The reason is that ______.”
“What do you think about my idea?”
Record yourself. Check: Did you say your idea clearly? Did you give a reason with because? Did you invite the other person to answer?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult sitting beside me, listen to my idea and my because reason, then tell me one thing to say more clearly.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Talk About Your Feelings
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Practise talking about a feeling. It is brave and healthy to share how you feel.
“Yesterday I felt ______ because ______.”
“When I feel sadness, I like to ______.”
“Something that makes me feel hopeful is ______.”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With my helper nearby, let me name a feeling from this unit, then gently check that my sentence about it is complete.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 — Imagine It!
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person says: “Imagine you can ______ (fly / talk to animals / be invisible).”
The other person answers: “If I could ______, I would ______, because ______.”
Then swap turns. Try to use a new idea each time!
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Play the Imagine It game with me while an adult is in the room, and give me a new imagine prompt on every turn.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Word Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words, such as idea, purpose, sense, sincere, hopeless and allow. Ask your partner one clear question about each word, then answer their six questions in full sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to stay with me, then quiz me on six unit words and tell me which answers used each word correctly.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain why sharing sadness can help a friend. Give one reason with because, then answer two follow-up questions from your listener without reading from your notes.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With my parent or teacher listening, ask me two follow-up questions about sharing sadness and check that I gave a reason.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — One-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan a one-minute talk about something you like to imagine. Open with your idea, add three details in order, and close with the reason it matters to you.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: While an adult supervises, time my one-minute talk about imagining, then tell me whether my three details stayed in order.

writing.title: Writing 1 — My Idea for the Box
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Imagine you can add one idea to the Box of Ideas. Write three or four sentences about your dream, memory, or hope. Try to use the words idea, imagine and because.
Start like this: “My idea is ______. I imagine ______. This is important to me because ______.”
writing.modelText: My idea is a reading garden for our class. I imagine soft cushions under the wide tree by the wall. This is important to me because quiet reading makes my whole day calmer.
writing.successCriteria: I wrote three or four sentences; I used idea, imagine and because; every sentence has a capital letter and an end mark; I read my paper aloud before folding it.
writing.support: Say your idea out loud first, then write it exactly as you said it.
writing.extension: Add one sentence that describes how your idea would make other children feel.

writing.title: Writing 2 — A Time I Used My Imagination
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write about a time you used your imagination to solve a problem or to feel better. What did you imagine? How did it help you? Use the simple past tense, such as imagined, allowed and felt.
writing.modelText: Last winter I felt hopeless about my broken kite. I imagined a new tail made from ribbon, and then I built it. My kite flew higher than before, so my heavy feeling melted away.
writing.successCriteria: I told the events in order; I used at least four past-tense verbs; I explained how imagining helped me; I checked every -ed ending.
writing.support: Draw three quick pictures of what happened, then write one sentence under each.
writing.extension: Add an irregular past-tense verb, such as went, saw or had, and underline it.

writing.title: Writing 3 — My Opinion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Answer this opinion question in three sentences: “Is it good to share our feelings, even our sadness?” Say what you believe, and give one clear reason.
writing.modelText: I believe it is good to share our sadness with somebody we trust. Talking makes the heavy feeling smaller, because a friend can listen and understand. I still choose the right moment, so nobody feels rushed.
writing.successCriteria: I opened with I believe; I gave one reason using because; I stayed on the question; I checked my full stops.
writing.support: Answer the question out loud first, then keep the words you liked best.
writing.extension: Add a sentence that answers somebody who disagrees with you.

writing.title: Writing 4 — Connected Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence paragraph about the Box of Ideas. Begin with a topic sentence, then join your ideas with and, but, so or because.
writing.modelText: The Box of Ideas holds one folded paper from every child in the class. Sami felt unsure, but Amal told him to imagine something better. He wrote about flying over the sea, and the whole class smiled. Teacher Yasmin sealed the box because the children will open it next year. Every paper inside it carries a real reason. That is why this box matters so much.
writing.successCriteria: My first sentence tells the reader my topic; I used three different joining words; my sentences follow one order; I reread and improved two of them.
writing.support: Write the six ideas as short notes first, then join pairs of notes together.
writing.extension: Replace two ordinary words with more precise unit words.

writing.title: Writing 5 — Information Text
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a heading and five true facts about abstract nouns for another Year 3 reader. Use precise unit vocabulary, and check your spelling and punctuation.
writing.modelText: Abstract Nouns: Five Facts. An abstract noun names something you cannot touch. Idea, sadness and experience are all abstract nouns. You can make one stronger with a describing word. Many of them grow from smaller words, such as sad and sadness. Writers need them to explain thoughts and feelings.
writing.successCriteria: My heading names the topic; each fact is true and different; I used at least four unit words; I proofread for spelling and full stops.
writing.support: Reuse the examples from Grammar 1 and put them into your own words.
writing.extension: Add a labelled diagram that sorts three abstract nouns from three concrete nouns.

writing.title: Writing 6 — Reflect and Improve
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose one earlier piece from this unit. Rewrite three sentences so they are clearer, add two precise details, and explain the single best improvement you made.
writing.modelText: I chose my Box of Ideas paper and rewrote three sentences. I changed nice to enjoyable, and I added the reason my idea matters to me. My best improvement was joining two short sentences with because.
writing.successCriteria: I rewrote three sentences; I added two precise details; I named my best improvement; I explained why it made the writing clearer.
writing.support: Read the old piece aloud and mark the sentence that sounded least clear.
writing.extension: Ask a partner to name one more change, then decide whether you agree.

activities.title: Activities 1 — True or False (Feelings and Ideas)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False for each sentence. If it is false, write the sentence correctly.
An idea is a thought or plan in your mind.
Sadness is a feeling we should never talk about.
To imagine means to picture something in your mind.
A reason tells us why something happens.
activities.answerSummary: Three sentences are true; only the sentence about never talking is false, and it must be rewritten kindly.

activities.title: Activities 2 — Choose the Feeling Word
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Choose the best word for each gap: sincere, thoughtful, hopeless, or enjoyable.
The class trip to the sea was very ______. We had so much fun.
She gave a ______ apology, straight from her heart.
Bringing a gift for a sick friend is a ______ thing to do.
When I could not find my shoe, I felt ______.
activities.answerSummary: Each gap takes one adjective only, and the clue sits in the second half of the sentence.

activities.title: Activities 3 — Add the Reason
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Finish each sentence with your own reason, using because.
I like to imagine, because ______.
I suggest we help the new student, because ______.
Sadness is not a bad thing, because ______.
activities.answerSummary: Answers will differ, so judge whether the reason truly explains the first half of the sentence.

activities.title: Activities 4 — Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort all sixteen unit words into thinking words, feeling words and sharing words. Then write one sentence for each group that explains the rule you used.
activities.answerSummary: Six thinking words, six feeling words and four sharing words, each group explained in one full sentence.

activities.title: Activities 5 — Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find six details in The Box of Ideas that show how Sami's feelings change. Copy each detail, then explain what it tells the reader.
activities.answerSummary: Details should move from unsure at the start to confident at the end, with a short explanation for each.

activities.title: Activities 6 — Create and Teach
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Design a short card game that teaches abstract nouns to a younger reader. Write the rules clearly, then play one round with your helper.
activities.answerSummary: Rules must be written as imperatives, and every scoring card must name a real abstract noun.

assignments.title: Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Submit two pieces. First, your revised Writing 1 paper for the Box of Ideas. Second, a recording of Speaking 1, in which you share an idea and give a reason. Marking guidance for the teacher: award up to 16 marks for the writing using the Grade 3 writing rubric, judging ideas, organisation, language and conventions; award up to 16 marks for the recording using the Grade 3 speaking rubric, judging clarity, vocabulary, sentence accuracy and interaction. Credit any paper that names a dream, a memory or a hope and explains it with because, and credit any recording in which the child states an idea, gives a reason and invites a reply. Return one specific improvement target for each piece.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: Which meaning belongs to the word purpose?
quizzes.options: The job that something is made for, or why a person does it. | A dark shape that appears when something blocks the sunlight. | The decision you make when you pick one thing from two or more. | Something that happened to you and stays inside your memory.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The job that something is made for, or why a person does it.
quizzes.explanation: Sami does not know the purpose of his idea, which means he cannot say what job it is meant to do.
quizzes.origin: Curriculum-reviewed item authored from this unit's texts

quizzes.question: In The Box of Ideas at School, which word completes this line: “Imagine something ______,” I told him?
quizzes.explanation: The narrator encourages Sami to picture a stronger idea, so the missing word is better.
quizzes.origin: Curriculum-reviewed item authored from this unit's texts

quizzes.question: Which one of these words is an abstract noun?
quizzes.explanation: You cannot see or touch sadness, while a playground, a teacher and an umbrella can all be touched.
quizzes.origin: Curriculum-reviewed item authored from this unit's texts

quizzes.question: Nora gave a sincere apology. What does sincere tell us about her?
quizzes.options: She spoke quickly and then walked away. | She said sorry in a very loud voice. | She truly meant the words she said. | She wrote her apology instead of saying it.
quizzes.correctAnswer: She truly meant the words she said.
quizzes.explanation: Sincere means honest and truly meaning what you say, so her apology came from the heart.
quizzes.origin: Curriculum-reviewed item authored from this unit's texts

quizzes.question: In A Group Discussion, Teacher Yasmin says: “Please ______ an idea, and allow everyone to speak.” Which word fits?
quizzes.explanation: She is asking the class to offer ideas, and the verb for offering an idea is suggest.
quizzes.origin: Curriculum-reviewed item authored from this unit's texts

quizzes.question: Which sentence uses its modal verb correctly?
quizzes.options: He cans swim across the pool. | She will swims to the island. | They musts listen to the teacher. | We should help the new student.
quizzes.correctAnswer: We should help the new student.
quizzes.explanation: A modal verb never adds -s, and the verb after it stays plain, so should help is the correct pair.
quizzes.origin: Curriculum-reviewed item authored from this unit's texts

quizzes.question: In The Box of Ideas, Amal says: “That story has a strong ______.” Which word completes her line?
quizzes.explanation: Amal is talking about why the writer once felt hopeless, and the word for that cause is reason.
quizzes.origin: Curriculum-reviewed item authored from this unit's texts

quizzes.question: Sami said, “I can imagine flying.” Which sentence reports his words correctly?
quizzes.options: Sami said I can imagine flying. | Sami said he can imagine flying. | Sami says that he could imagine flying. | Sami said he could imagine flying.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Sami said he could imagine flying.
quizzes.explanation: Reported speech moves I to he and can to could, while the reporting verb said stays in the past.
quizzes.origin: Curriculum-reviewed item authored from this unit's texts

quizzes.question: In Feelings Are Not Bad or Good, why does the writer compare sadness with rain?
quizzes.options: To show that sadness is a feeling, just as rain is a kind of weather. | To warn readers that a rainy day will always make a child feel sad and lonely. | To explain how clouds and puddles are made in the sky. | To show that sadness lasts exactly as long as a rain shower.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To show that sadness is a feeling, just as rain is a kind of weather.
quizzes.explanation: The text calls rain a kind of weather, so the comparison makes sadness ordinary rather than bad.
quizzes.origin: Curriculum-reviewed item authored from this unit's texts

quizzes.question: Which sentence joins its two ideas with the correct conjunction?
quizzes.options: I enjoy thinking, or it helps me to learn new things. | It was raining, because we stayed inside. | I enjoy thinking because it helps me learn. | Do you want juice, so milk?
quizzes.correctAnswer: I enjoy thinking because it helps me learn.
quizzes.explanation: Because gives a reason, so it is the right glue between enjoying thinking and learning something.
quizzes.origin: Curriculum-reviewed item authored from this unit's texts

liveSessions.title: Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination: Launch, vocabulary and learning goals
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read the unit overview and learn the six thinking words in Group 1.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min unit launch and word wall; 10 min teacher models idea, reason and purpose; 15 min paired meaning match; 10 min each child says one idea with a reason; 5 min goal setting.
liveSessions.afterSession: Add three thinking words to your word wall and use two of them at home.

liveSessions.title: Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination: Reading fluency and comprehension
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read The Box of Ideas at School aloud twice and underline any hard word.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min fluency warm-up; 10 min shared reading of the story opening; 15 min paired evidence hunt; 10 min answering inference questions together; 5 min reflection on one new word.
liveSessions.afterSession: Answer comprehension questions 1 to 6 and check them with your helper.

liveSessions.title: Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination: Grammar workshop and feedback
liveSessions.beforeSession: Complete the abstract noun practice and the countable-uncountable practice.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min retrieval quiz on abstract nouns; 10 min modelling modal verbs; 15 min sorting countable and uncountable nouns; 10 min sentence building; 5 min feedback on the most common slips.
liveSessions.afterSession: Correct your grammar practice using the answer key in the teacher notes.

liveSessions.title: Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination: Dialogue, speaking and pronunciation
liveSessions.beforeSession: Prepare one idea and one reason for the class garden discussion.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min listening to the group discussion; 10 min modelling suggest and allow; 15 min group planning with turn-taking roles; 10 min recording Speaking 1; 5 min peer praise.
liveSessions.afterSession: Record Speaking 1 with an adult nearby and listen back once.

liveSessions.title: Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination: Writing conference and revision
liveSessions.beforeSession: Draft your Box of Ideas paper for Writing 1.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min sharing drafts; 10 min modelling a because sentence; 15 min individual writing conferences; 10 min revising three sentences; 5 min setting one improvement target.
liveSessions.afterSession: Revise Writing 1 using the improvement target from your conference.

liveSessions.title: Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination: Performance, assessment and reflection
liveSessions.beforeSession: Practise reading your finished paper aloud with expression.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min voice warm-up; 10 min performing the Box of Ideas papers; 15 min unit quiz; 10 min self-assessment; 5 min celebrating the end of Year 3.
liveSessions.afterSession: Complete the unit quiz and the self-assessment, then tick your word wall.

teacherNotes.note: Model each abstract noun aloud and check meaning before independent work, because these words name things a child cannot point at. Offer spoken, drawn or written responses so every learner can show understanding. Keep an adult present whenever a child records audio or works with the AI tutor, and never leave a young learner alone with the device. Feelings talk can surface real worries, so agree in advance who the child may go to, and give feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and precise vocabulary.

teacherNotes.noteType: Source answer key and AI tutor support
teacherNotes.note:
Grammar answer key.
Abstract Nouns: 1. courage, 2. happiness, 3. idea, 4. sadness.
Modal Verbs and the Future with Will: 1. must, 2. should, 3. will, 4. Can; then 1. will play, 2. will not go, 3. will help.
Imperatives and Opinion Questions: 1. Help, 2. Don't interrupt, 3. Share, 4. Be; then 1. What, 2. Is, 3. Do.
Countable and Uncountable Nouns: 1. a reason, 2. some water, 3. much experience.
Reported Speech: 1. He said he loved ice cream. 2. She said she would come to the game. 3. They said they were happy. 4. Sami said he could imagine flying.
Conjunctions and the Simple Past: 1. but, 2. but, 3. because, 4. because; then 1. imagined, 2. allowed, 3. saw.
Ask your AI tutor, with an adult beside you:
“Can you read the Box of Ideas text with me and explain any hard words?”
“Can you play a listening game where I tell you what a person said?”
“Can you help me use idea, reason and purpose in sentences?”
“What is the difference between sincere and thoughtful?”
“Can you give me the opposite of hopeless?”
“Can you practise a group discussion with me where we both suggest ideas?”
“Can you check my grammar answers and my writing?”
“Can you help me change what someone said into reported speech?”

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: 1. True. 2. False, so rewrite it as: sadness is a feeling we can share with someone we trust. 3. True. 4. True.
1. enjoyable, 2. sincere, 3. thoughtful, 4. hopeless. Ask the child which clue word pointed to each answer.
Reasons differ. Accept any reason that genuinely explains the first half, and check that because still joins the two parts.
Thinking: idea, imagine, reason, purpose, choice, sense. Feeling: sadness, experience, sincere, thoughtful, hopeless, enjoyable. Sharing: allow, suggest, enjoy, believe.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Look for six of these: he did not smile; he said his idea was not good; he did not know its purpose; he still felt unsure; he smiled at last; he whispered that he now believes.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Accept any playable card game whose rules are written as commands and whose scoring cards each name a real abstract noun.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: The job that something is made for, or why a person does it. Purpose answers what an object or plan is for.
Better. The narrator wants Sami to picture a stronger idea, not a quieter or a later one.
Sadness. It is the only word on the list that names something you cannot touch.
She truly meant the words she said. A sincere speaker means what they say.
Suggest. Teacher Yasmin is inviting ideas, and suggest is the verb for offering one.
We should help the new student. Only this option keeps the modal and the following verb plain.
Reason. Amal is naming the cause behind the writer's feeling, not its purpose.
Sami said he could imagine flying. Both the pronoun and the modal move back one step.
To show that sadness is a feeling, just as rain is a kind of weather. The comparison makes sadness normal.
I enjoy thinking because it helps me learn. Because is the joining word that gives a reason.

selfAssessment.statement: I can use abstract nouns like idea, reason, and sadness.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can say what I imagine.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can give a reason using “because”.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use “can” and “will” to talk about the future.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can suggest an idea and let others speak.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can tell someone what another person said.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself
